updates.q: How long should clarifying questions take in a 60-min decomp round?
updates.a: 10-15 minutes. Most candidates rush in under 3 minutes. The interviewer is testing whether you can stay in the problem space before solving -- senior candidates linger longer than feels comfortable.

updates.q: I -> L -> O mental model -- what goes where?
updates.a: Inputs = data sources (logs, APIs, files). Logic = transformations, decisions, joins. Outputs = what the user sees or acts on (dashboards, alerts, reports).

(1) 'Before I propose anything...' (2) 'The simplest version that delivers value...' (3) 'X over Y because of this specific constraint -- if it changes I'd flip.' (4) 'Let me make an explicit assumption: X; please push back.'

updates.q: Senior signal: how long should clarifying-question time feel uncomfortable?
updates.a: Past the point where you want to start drawing -- typically 10-15 min in a 60-min round. The discomfort is the signal you're doing it right; juniors leave step 1 the moment they have a plausible solution.

updates.q: When interviewer says 'you decide', what do you do?
updates.a: Make an explicit assumption out loud and invite correction: 'I'll assume X -- please push back if wrong.' Write it on the board, then proceed. Asking back ('what would you prefer?') signals you cannot operate under ambiguity.

updates.q: The 'decomposition shape' -- how many solution options should you present?
updates.a: TWO phased options with explicit tradeoffs plus a recommendation and rationale. One option signals no tradeoff awareness; three or more signals indecision.

updates.q: A senior signal phrase in decomp?
updates.a: 'X over Y because of this specific constraint -- if it changes I'd flip the decision.' Explicit tradeoff plus conditional commitment.

updates.q: Hospital adoption (12% after 90 days) -- first analytical split?
updates.a: Product gaps vs adoption gaps. Different fixes: product needs product changes; adoption needs training, change management, or incentives. Naming the split before proposing fixes is the senior move.

updates.q: What to draw FIRST on the whiteboard?
updates.a: Inputs -> Logic -> Outputs. Boxes describe BEHAVIOR (not tech) until you discuss implementation choices. Naming Kafka or Airflow before scoping is an instant-reject signal.

new_cards.q: Approximate time budget per phase in a 60-min decomp round?
new_cards.a: Clarify 10-15 min, stakeholders 3 min, data sources 5 min, tradeoffs 10 min, failure modes 5 min, with buffer for whiteboard and questions. The shape matters more than exact minutes -- clarify and tradeoffs should dominate.

new_cards.q: Three properties of a 'good' clarifying question?
new_cards.a: (1) Specific -- names a concrete dimension (latency, user, frequency). (2) Falsifiable -- has a yes/no or A/B answer. (3) Design-relevant -- the answer changes what you would build next. Vague questions like 'what does success look like?' fail all three.

new_cards.q: The 'I don't know -- you decide' trap and the correct response?
new_cards.a: Trap: bouncing ambiguity back to the interviewer ('what would you prefer?') signals you cannot operate without a spec. Correct: state an explicit assumption out loud, write it on the board, proceed, and invite pushback ('I'll assume daily batch; flag me if real-time').

new_cards.q: What is the decomp interviewer actually grading?
new_cards.a: HOW you think under ambiguity, not whether you pick the 'right' architecture. Signal: clarify before solving, name stakeholders, surface tradeoffs, anticipate failure modes. Knowing the trendy tool is irrelevant if you skipped the problem space.

new_cards.q: Junior vs senior signal patterns in decomp?
new_cards.a: Junior: names tools first (Kafka, Postgres, OpenAI API), proposes one solution, treats the prompt as complete. Senior: scopes users and constraints, names two phased options with tradeoffs, calls out failure modes and rollback, and explicitly states assumptions.

new_cards.q: Origin and current spread of the decomposition interview format?
new_cards.a: Pioneered by Palantir for Forward Deployed Engineer hiring; now standard at OpenAI, Anthropic, and Scale AI for applied and research-engineering roles. The shared bet: messy real-world problems reveal judgment that LeetCode cannot.

new_cards.q: When should you draw architecture vs stay verbal in a decomp round?
new_cards.a: Stay verbal during clarify and stakeholder mapping. Draw Inputs -> Logic -> Outputs once you have a scoped problem -- the diagram anchors tradeoff discussion. Drawing too early commits you to a shape before you understand the requirements.

(1) Tech-stack-first ('I'd use Spark and Snowflake...') before scoping users. (2) Naming tools as solutions ('use an LLM') without specifying inputs and outputs. (3) Single-solution presentation with no tradeoff or alternative. All three signal a coder, not an engineer.

new_cards.q: How do you handle a silent interviewer in a decomp round?
new_cards.a: Treat silence as a signal to keep thinking out loud and check in: 'I'm going to assume X and move to data sources -- stop me if you want me to dig deeper here.' Silence is rarely disapproval; it is usually 'show me more of your reasoning'.

new_cards.q: Why MVP-first sequencing in a decomp answer?
new_cards.a: Pitching the simplest version that delivers value first proves you can ship under constraints; layering enhancements after proves you understand the roadmap. Leading with the fully-featured system signals you cannot prioritize and have not internalized cost.

new_cards.q: Logistics rerouting agent -- three failure modes worth naming?
new_cards.a: (1) GPS dropout in tunnels or rural zones -- detect with stale-timestamp checks, fall back to last known route. (2) Model bias toward training-distribution cities -- monitor reroute success rate by region. (3) Cascading reroutes during incidents -- rate-limit and require human approval above a threshold.

new_cards.q: Failure-mode framing for low-base-rate events (fraud, medical alerts)?
new_cards.a: Lead with the false-positive cost, not just accuracy. At 0.1% base rate a 99% accurate model still drowns reviewers in false alarms. Name the precision target, the human-in-the-loop step, and the detect-and-rollback mechanism for model drift.

new_cards.q: What does a stakeholder map add beyond a user list?
new_cards.a: Users are who interacts with the system; stakeholders include who pays, who is regulated, who is affected, and who can kill the project. Naming the procurement lead, compliance officer, and on-call engineer scopes the problem in ways a pure user-story view misses.
